# Evaluating AGENTS.md: Are Repository-Level Context Files Helpful for Coding Agents?

## Core Thesis

Repository-level context files did not reliably help coding agents in these evaluations. The aggregate result tilted negative versus giving no repository context, while developer-written files performed somewhat better than LLM-generated ones. The main pattern is not that extra repository guidance is useless in every setting, but that added context often changed agent behavior in expensive ways without clearly improving task completion.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This pushes against the easy assumption that more repository guidance automatically improves autonomous coding. For Sapho’s evaluation doctrine, it argues for testing context artifacts as interventions rather than treating them as free capability multipliers. It also sharpens a broader operating lesson: guidance that increases search, tool use, and token spend may still fail if it does not move the agent faster toward the right files and actions. The paper matters because it reframes repository context as a bounded tradeoff among success, cost, and behavioral steering, not a default best practice.

## Key Findings

- Across the reported evaluations, supplying repository-level context files tended to lower coding-agent task success relative to giving no repository context.
- Developer-provided context files performed better than LLM-generated ones on average: developer-written files improved performance by 4% relative to no context, while LLM-generated files reduced performance by 3%.
- LLM-generated context files reduced average resolution rate by 0.5% on SWE-bench Lite and 2% on AGENTbench.
- Repository-level context files raised inference cost substantially, with reported average increases above 20%; LLM-generated files increased average cost by 20% on SWE-bench Lite and 23% on AGENTbench.
- Context-file conditions also increased agent activity: LLM-generated files added 2.45 average steps on SWE-bench Lite and 3.92 on AGENTbench, while developer-provided files increased average step count by 3.34 and raised cost by as much as 19%.
- The behavioral shift was visible in tool and repository interaction: with context files present, agents ran more tests, searched more files, read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling.
- Repository context did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before agents first touched a file that was actually modified in the original pull request patch.
- Agents strongly followed explicit tool cues: when uv was mentioned in the context file, agents used it about 1.6 times per instance versus fewer than 0.01 times when it was not mentioned.

## Evidence and Findings

- The paper’s top-line result is that repository-level context files tended to reduce task success relative to no repository context across multiple coding agents and models. That supports a disciplined negative aggregate judgment: context files were not an across-the-board performance aid in the tested setup.
- The comparison between human-written and model-written context is qualified rather than simple. Developer-provided files improved performance by 4% relative to no context, while LLM-generated files reduced performance by 3%, and developer-written files outperformed model-written ones for all four evaluated agents. This supports the narrower conclusion that file quality or structure matters, not the broader claim that context files are generally beneficial.
- The cost penalty is substantial and concrete. LLM-generated context files increased average cost by 20% on SWE-bench Lite and 23% on AGENTbench, while also increasing average steps by 2.45 and 3.92 respectively. Reported reasoning-token use also rose: by 22% and 14% for GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.1 mini on SWE-bench Lite, and by 14% and 10% on AGENTbench. The relevant implication is that added repository context materially increases spend and agent work even when success does not improve.
- The mechanism is behavioral steering, but with weak evidence of navigational benefit. With context files present, agents ran more tests, searched and read more files, wrote more files, and used more repository-specific tooling. Yet they did not meaningfully reduce the number of steps before first interacting with files that actually mattered to the original patch. That matters because it suggests the extra activity was often exploration overhead rather than faster localization of the fix.
- The traces make that steering effect concrete. Manual inspection found GPT-5.1 mini often spent extra steps locating and rereading context files already present in its context, and explicit instructions inside those files were followed strongly, as shown by uv usage jumping to about 1.6 times per instance when mentioned versus fewer than 0.01 times otherwise. This supports a bounded conclusion that context files shape behavior reliably, but not necessarily productively.
- The benchmark itself is nontrivial but bounded. AGENTbench contains 138 instances from 12 repositories with developer-written context files; the authors retained 87% of filtered pull requests after requiring machine-readable test results with at least one passing test, and generated tests achieved 75% average coverage of modified code. These details make the evaluation more concrete, but they also define the scope of what the paper can claim.

## Contradictions and Tensions

The main tension is internal to the results. The aggregate finding says repository-level context files tended to hurt success relative to no context, but the subset comparison says developer-provided files improved performance by 4% relative to no context and clearly beat LLM-generated files. That means “context files hurt” is too blunt; the paper supports a more conditional judgment about heterogeneous context quality and use.

There is also a cost-performance tension. Context files increased cost, steps, reasoning-token use, and repository-specific activity, but that extra work did not clearly shorten the path to relevant files or consistently raise success. More guided behavior was visible, but more effective behavior was not.

A further tension appears in the altered documentation-sparse setting: when other documentation was removed, LLM-generated context files improved performance by 2.7% on average and outperformed developer-written documentation. That result does not erase the broader negative aggregate finding, but it does show that the same kind of context artifact can become useful when the surrounding repository is less documented.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The best-supported mechanism is that context files steer agent behavior toward repository-specific exploration and instruction-following. Agents searched, read, tested, and invoked tools more when context files were present, and they strongly adopted named tools such as uv when prompted. That is a real behavioral effect.

What the evidence does not show is a single general causal story for why this steering usually failed to improve outcomes. The bounded explanation is that context files often induced extra repository work without reliably helping agents identify the relevant files sooner. In some cases the files may have added redundant reading or broadened the search space; in documentation-sparse settings, the same added guidance may have become genuinely useful. The mechanism is therefore situational and benchmark-bound rather than universal.

## Limits

The evidence does not justify a blanket claim that repository context is harmful in all coding-agent settings. The strongest negative result is aggregate and sits alongside a positive developer-written subset and a positive ablation under reduced-documentation conditions.

The paper also does not isolate which properties of developer-written files made them better than LLM-generated ones. Better performance could reflect relevance, brevity, structure, instruction quality, or alignment with repository reality, but this evaluation does not separate those factors.

Some mechanistic evidence comes from trace inspection and behavioral metrics rather than controlled causal intervention. That makes the steering story plausible and supported, but still incomplete.
